findings.title: Saved dwell fraction counts off-target wall-clock time â€” a glance back commits a click in 1-3 frames
findings.scenario: Papa dwells on YouTube card A for ~700ms (real progress ~51%), then his gaze drifts slowly off the card into dead page space (header, margins >230px from any card). Because the held path only checks frame-to-frame velocity, the dwell clock keeps running with state.targetKey still = A while clickReq is null (no commit possible, no reset). 500ms later a tremor jump (>60px) breaks stability â€” the save block computes frac from the inflated clock: min(0.99, (1200-280)/820) = 0.99, attributed to card A. Within the 1s TTL he glances back at the card to re-read its title: the next low-velocity frame resumes at 99% (resumeElapsed = 1091.8ms of dwellMs 1100), onset is skipped, and the click commits on the following frame â€” ~15-45ms after his gaze lands, faster than human reaction time, with no ring warning. He is navigated to a video he explicitly drifted away from without selecting.
findings.evidence: The held path is entered purely on velocity: `const stabilityHeld = dist < stabilityRadius || (sameTarget && insideTargetRegion);` (L1288) â€” with clickReq null, radius is baseStability 60px, easily satisfied by WMA(3)-smoothed drift (commit 9dc3c44 smooths this exact input), and nothing in the held path clears the stale targetKey or stops the clock (commit at L1491 requires clickReq; onset-cancel at L1401 requires clickReq). The save block then trusts that clock: `const elapsedAtBreak = now - state.start; if (elapsedAtBreak > onsetMs) { const frac = Math.min(0.99, (elapsedAtBreak - onsetMs) / Math.max(1, dwellMs - onsetMs));` (L1312-1315) â€” there is no check that gaze was on the target during that elapsed time, and the clamp to 0.99 converts an arbitrarily overrun clock into a near-complete save. The commit message claims 'the same savedDwellRef semantics the main-app cursor has had since v10' â€” false on both counts: GazeCursor.tsx only accumulates progress while the hit-tested element is the active dwell target with onset completed (L830-835), and it refuses to save overrun progress: `if (currentProgress >= FIXATION_TTL_MIN_PROGRESS && currentProgress < 1)` (L836) â€” overrun is discarded in the app, saved as 0.99 in the browser. Resume math: `const resumeElapsed = state.savedProgress * Math.max(1, dwellMs - onsetMs) + onsetMs; state.start = now - resumeElapsed;` (L1383-1384) leaves only 8.2ms to commit at frac 0.99 â€” one 66Hz frame.

findings.title: sticky_resume ghost fabricates the key match: save resumes and commits on stale coordinates after a DOM reflow, bypassing the v17.15 onset-movement cancel
findings.scenario: Papa is 70% through a dwell on card A when a tremor excursion breaks stability but stays within A's rect+80px halo. YouTube inserts an ad / lazy-loads content, relocating card A (no navigation event fires, so the did-navigate gcResetDwell invalidation never runs; the MutationObserver only bumps posterior epochs â€” bumpEpoch never touches savedProgress/targetKey/targetRect). His gaze settles back in the halo: resolveClickRequest returns null (card A moved away, the new occupant is an ad iframe / loading skeleton that resolves to nothing), so the ghost synthesizes clickReq with the SAVED key and the STALE rect. The break path sets targetKey = ghost key = savedProgressKey (the different-target invalidation at L1345 is skipped because keys match), the next frame resumes at 70%, and ~250ms later the commit returns the ghost clickReq whose x/y are the stale rect center. main.ts sendTrustedBrowserClick(cx, cy) dispatches raw mouseDown/mouseUp at those coordinates â€” clicking the ad that now occupies the space. The patient is navigated to an advertiser site and must gaze-navigate back.
findings.evidence: Ghost: `clickReq = { ... kind: 'sticky_resume', key: state.targetKey, ... rect: sRect }` (L1251-1259) where sRect = state.targetRect, a snapshot that nothing refreshes when the real element is gone (L1432 'refresh' assigns clickReq.rect â€” which IS the same stale state.targetRect). The v17.15 onset-movement cancel that was built exactly for 'a YouTube re-flow mid-dwell (lazy thumbnail, ad insertion) drags the commit onto whichever card landed under the gaze' (comment L1398-1400) compares state.onsetTargetRect against clickReq.rect (L1401-1413) â€” stale-vs-stale, shiftPx â‰ˆ 0, never fires. The app mechanism this commit claims to mirror cannot be fooled this way: GazeCursor.tsx resume requires DOM identity `savedDwellRef.current.element === clickable` (L899-900) on a freshly hit-tested element. Pre-existing exposure was bounded: the ghost could only commit on a stale rect after a full fresh 1100ms re-dwell (sustained deliberate stare); the save shrinks the patient's reaction window to the remaining (1-frac)*820ms â€” as little as one frame â€” after a single re-entry into the halo.

findings.title: Retention silently fails when the same card is re-acquired via a different resolution path â€” kind is baked into the key
findings.scenario: Papa dwells to 75% on a card with gaze on the thumbnail (direct elementFromPoint hit â†’ key kind 'youtube_anchor'). A tremor excursion breaks stability and the save is recorded under that key. His gaze returns NEAR the card but lands just off the element in the snap zone â€” the canonical tremor re-acquisition this feature was built for â€” so resolution comes from bayesianYoutubeCard/nearestYoutubeCard with kind 'youtube_nearest_card'. The key string differs (kind is the first component of stableKeyFor), so line 1345 treats it as a DIFFERENT target and deletes the save. The dwell restarts from 0 â€” the exact 60-80%-then-restart failure mode the commit message says it fixes persists in the near-miss re-acquisition path, making the feature's behavior feel random to the patient.
findings.evidence: `const stableKeyFor = (target, kind, href, label, rect) => [kind, href || '', ...]` (L578) â€” same anchor element yields three distinct keys depending on resolution path ('youtube_anchor' L686, 'youtube_card' L690, 'youtube_nearest_card' L743/L961). The codebase already knows this: isLockedYoutubeCard (L710-718) explicitly compares all three kind-variants against state.targetKey. The new invalidation does an exact string compare with no such normalization: `if (newTargetKey && state.savedProgressKey && newTargetKey !== state.savedProgressKey) { state.savedProgress = 0; ... }` (L1345-1349). The resume gate (L1380) is likewise exact-match, so a kind-flip can never resume â€” only invalidate.

findings.title: Documented rollback (gcConfig.progressRetentionEnabled = false) silently re-arms on every full page load
findings.line: ~164 (default true); electron/main.ts ~1320-1331 (seedConfig omits flag), ~1742-1760 (setGazeConfig whitelist omits flag)
findings.scenario: After observing resume misfires on the rig, the caregiver follows the commit's documented rollback: sets window.gcConfig.progressRetentionEnabled = false in the BrowserView DevTools. It works â€” until Papa opens a different site or the browser screen is closed and reopened: every full (non-SPA) navigation creates a fresh JS context, the injection IIFE re-runs with `progressRetentionEnabled: true` in its defaults, and the rollback silently evaporates. The feature the team believed was disabled is live again with no indication, and the misfires resume.
findings.evidence: The flag exists only inside the injected page context: defaults `progressRetentionEnabled: true` (browserGazeController.ts L164) are merged via `window.gcConfig = Object.assign({ ...defaults }, window.gcConfig || {})` (L122-172) â€” empty on a fresh document. There is no main-process backing: seedConfig in injectBrowserPageHelpers (main.ts L1320-1331) enumerates dwellMs/onsetMs/radii only, and the `webview:setGazeConfig` IPC handler (main.ts L1742+) rebuilds browserGazeConfig from a fixed whitelist that has no progressRetention key. So unlike dwellMs (which the seed restores per page), the rollback flag has no persistence or re-seed path â€” 'Rollback without code edits' as documented only holds for the lifetime of one renderer context. The same pattern affects gapPauseEnabled from d9fb816.
